text: The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg for a robotic total DII gastrectomy and Roux-en-Y esophago-jejunostomy. Ports are placed for access, and the liver is retracted using a gauze suture. Near infrared vision (NIR) is utilized to visualize the tumor, highlighted by endocyanine green injection, and to identify draining lymph nodes during the procedure.

The surgeon performs a partial omentectomy, starting dissection 4 cm from the greater curvature towards the spleen. The upper border of the pancreas is dissected to access station eleven lymph nodes. Dissection continues to the short gastric vessels, dissecting the left gastroepiploic vessels at their roots. The gastrosplenic ligament is separated up to the left side of the esophageal hiatus by dividing the short gastric vessels from the spleen's surface to dissect four SA lymph nodes. Station eleven D and ten lymph nodes at the splenic hilum are illuminated and cleared using near-infrared vision, showing no remaining lymph nodes.

The surgeon continues the division of the gastric colic ligament distally towards the pylorus, identifying and dividing the right gastroepiploic vessels at their roots. A swab is placed behind the duodenum, visible during anterior caucarization. The duodenum is transected distal to the pylorus using a linear stapler. The lesser omentum is opened and divided up to the right side of the esophageal hiatus, exposing and dividing the right gastric artery at its origin. Soft tissues around the proper hepatic artery and medial side of the portal vein are cleared to facilitate dissection of stations VIIIa and XIIa. The left gastric vein is exposed and divided at the upper margin of the common hepatic artery, followed by the left gastric artery at its origin. The retroperitoneal attachment of the stomach is detached up to the right crus, and the abdominal esophagus is mobilized with the vagus nerves divided. The distal esophagus is transected with a linear stapler, and the specimen is placed in a bag.

The surgeon delivers a loop of proximal jejunum towards the esophagus and performs an anticholic esophagojejunostomy using a linear stapler. The enterotomy is stapled closed, and another enterotomy is created for the jejuno-jejunostomy, located fifty centimeters distal to the esophagojejunostomy anastomosis.

The surgeon disconnects the biliopancreatic limb from the OJ anastomosis and positions it for the stapled JJ anastomosis. The enterotomy is closed using a stapler. Mesenteric defects are sutured closed, and a drain is placed in situ. The specimen is retrieved through a four-centimeter incision.
